title: Cross-Cutting Welsh Employment Framework Briefing
scenarioTrigger: User indicates they operate in Wales, employ staff in Wales, have Welsh-language obligations, or asks about devolved employment duties. Also triggered by queries about Welsh Language Standards, Welsh public sector equality duties, agricultural employment in Wales, or the Social Partnership Act.
constitutionalNote: Employment law is a reserved matter under the Government of Wales Act 2006 Schedule 7A. The UK Parliament retains legislative competence over employment rights, trade unions, industrial relations, and employment tribunals. However, the Senedd Cymru (Welsh Parliament) has enacted legislation in areas adjacent to employment — including the Welsh language, equality duties, agriculture, and public body governance — which creates additional obligations for employers operating in Wales. This briefing maps those additional Welsh-specific obligations.
constitutionalNoteCy: Mae cyfraith cyflogaeth yn fater a gadwyd o dan Ddeddf Llywodraeth Cymru 2006 Atodlen 7A. Mae Senedd y DU yn cadw cymhwysedd deddfwriaethol dros hawliau cyflogaeth, undebau llafur, cysylltiadau diwydiannol, a thribiwnlysoedd cyflogaeth. Fodd bynnag, mae Senedd Cymru wedi deddfu mewn meysydd sy'n gyfagos i gyflogaeth — gan gynnwys yr iaith Gymraeg, dyletswyddau cydraddoldeb, amaethyddiaeth, a llywodraethu cyrff cyhoeddus — sy'n creu rhwymedigaethau ychwanegol i gyflogwyr sy'n gweithredu yng Nghymru.

metadata.bilingualStatus: machine-generated — pending qualified Welsh-language professional review before deployment. All Welsh-language text in this file (titleCy, summaryCy, constitutionalNoteCy, briefingIntroductionCy) must be professionally reviewed before any client-facing use, in accordance with standing instructions.

briefingIntroduction: Employers operating in Wales face additional obligations beyond the UK-wide employment law framework. While employment law is reserved to the UK Parliament, the Senedd Cymru has legislated in adjacent areas that create employment-relevant duties — particularly for public bodies and organisations operating in Welsh-regulated sectors. This briefing identifies the key Welsh-specific instruments and explains how they interact with UK employment law.
briefingIntroductionCy: Mae cyflogwyr sy'n gweithredu yng Nghymru yn wynebu rhwymedigaethau ychwanegol y tu hwnt i fframwaith cyfraith cyflogaeth y DU gyfan. Er bod cyfraith cyflogaeth wedi'i chadw i Senedd y DU, mae Senedd Cymru wedi deddfu mewn meysydd cyfagos sy'n creu dyletswyddau sy'n berthnasol i gyflogaeth — yn enwedig ar gyfer cyrff cyhoeddus a sefydliadau sy'n gweithredu mewn sectorau a reoleiddir gan Gymru.

parts.title: Reserved vs Devolved: The Constitutional Framework
parts.titleCy: Materion a Gadwyd yn erbyn Materion Datganoledig: Y Fframwaith Cyfansoddiadol

sections.title: Employment law as a reserved matter
sections.titleCy: Cyfraith cyflogaeth fel mater a gadwyd
sections.summary: Employment law is a reserved matter under the Government of Wales Act 2006 Schedule 7A. This means that the UK Parliament retains legislative competence over: employment rights and duties (ERA 1996, EqA 2010), trade unions and industrial relations (TULRCA 1992), employment tribunals and the Employment Appeal Tribunal, the national minimum wage (NMWA 1998), health and safety at work (HSWA 1974), and TUPE (2006). The Senedd Cymru cannot legislate in these areas.
sections.summaryCy: Mae cyfraith cyflogaeth yn fater a gadwyd o dan Ddeddf Llywodraeth Cymru 2006 Atodlen 7A. Mae hyn yn golygu bod Senedd y DU yn cadw cymhwysedd deddfwriaethol dros: hawliau a dyletswyddau cyflogaeth, undebau llafur a chysylltiadau diwydiannol, tribiwnlysoedd cyflogaeth, yr isafswm cyflog cenedlaethol, iechyd a diogelwch yn y gwaith, a TUPE.
sections.text: Section 108A of and Schedule 7A to the Government of Wales Act 2006 (inserted by the Wales Act 2017) establish the reserved powers model for Wales. Employment and industrial relations are listed as reserved matters, meaning the Senedd may not legislate in these areas unless the legislation relates to or is ancillary to a devolved subject.
refs: Government of Wales Act 2006 Schedule 7A (reserved matters)
Wales Act 2017 (establishing the reserved powers model)
ERA 1996, EqA 2010, TULRCA 1992, NMWA 1998, HSWA 1974, TUPE 2006 — all UK-wide
commonErrors: Assuming that because employment law is reserved, the Senedd has no impact on employers in Wales — multiple Senedd Acts create additional obligations in adjacent areas including Welsh language, equality duties, agriculture, social partnership, and public body governance

Not recognising that the devolution boundary is complex — the Senedd can legislate on devolved matters (e.g., health, education, agriculture, Welsh language) even where the legislation has employment consequences, provided the primary purpose falls within devolved competence

Overlooking that some UK-wide employment provisions have been commenced in Wales but not England (e.g., EqA 2010 s.1 socio-economic duty) — Welsh employers may have obligations that English employers do not

sections.title: Welsh Language (Wales) Measure 2011
sections.summary: The Welsh Language (Wales) Measure 2011 established the office of the Welsh Language Commissioner (Comisiynydd y Gymraeg) and the system of Welsh Language Standards. Standards impose duties on specified organisations to provide services in Welsh, use Welsh internally, make Welsh-language policy decisions, promote the Welsh language, and keep records. From an employment law perspective, the Standards affect recruitment (bilingual job advertisements), workplace language policies, internal communications, and employee training.
sections.summaryCy: Sefydlodd Mesur y Gymraeg (Cymru) 2011 swyddfa Comisiynydd y Gymraeg a'r system o Safonau'r Gymraeg. Mae Safonau'n gosod dyletswyddau ar sefydliadau penodedig i ddarparu gwasanaethau yn Gymraeg, defnyddio'r Gymraeg yn fewnol, gwneud penderfyniadau polisi iaith Gymraeg, hyrwyddo'r Gymraeg, a chadw cofnodion.
sections.text: The Welsh Language (Wales) Measure 2011 received Royal Assent on 9 February 2011. Section 25 provides that the Welsh Language Commissioner may specify standards in relation to the Welsh language. Standards may relate to: service delivery (Part 2), policy making (Part 3), operational matters (Part 4), promotion (Part 5), and record keeping (Part 6).
sections.keyPrinciple: Welsh Language Standards are legally enforceable duties. Non-compliance may result in enforcement action by the Welsh Language Commissioner, including civil penalties. Employers subject to Standards must ensure their employment practices — recruitment, internal communications, training — comply with the applicable operational and service delivery standards.

standardsRegulations.title: Welsh Language Standards (No. 1) Regulations 2015
standardsRegulations.applicableTo: County and county borough councils, National Park authorities, fire and rescue authorities

standardsRegulations.title: Welsh Language Standards (No. 2) Regulations 2016
standardsRegulations.applicableTo: NHS bodies including local health boards, NHS trusts, and Public Health Wales

standardsRegulations.title: Welsh Language Standards (No. 5) Regulations 2016
standardsRegulations.applicableTo: Police forces, police and crime commissioners, British Transport Police (Welsh operations)

standardsRegulations.title: Welsh Language Standards (No. 7) Regulations 2018
standardsRegulations.applicableTo: Health regulators, Natural Resources Wales, qualifications bodies, higher education funding council

leadingCases.name: Gwynedd Council v Barratt and Hughes
leadingCases.principle: A requirement for Welsh language skills in a job specification can constitute indirect race or national origin discrimination under EqA 2010 s.19 if it disproportionately disadvantages candidates of a particular national origin and is not a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT considered whether the requirement for Welsh language fluency in a council job specification constituted indirect discrimination on grounds of national origin. The tribunal found that persons of non-Welsh national origin were at a particular disadvantage. The justification defence depended on whether the language requirement was proportionate to the legitimate aim of providing a bilingual service — which required a genuine operational need for the specific role, not merely a blanket policy.
leadingCases.significance: The leading EAT authority on the interaction between Welsh language requirements in employment and indirect national origin discrimination under the Equality Act 2010. Employers must demonstrate that a Welsh language requirement for a specific role is proportionate to a legitimate aim, assessed on the facts of each role — a blanket policy applying Welsh language requirements to all roles without role-specific justification carries significant discrimination risk.

refs: Welsh Language (Wales) Measure 2011 (mwa/2011/1)
Welsh Language Standards (various regulations specifying standards for different categories of organisation)
Welsh Language Commissioner — enforcement powers under Part 5 of the Measure
EqA 2010 s.9 (race as a protected characteristic — Welsh language speakers are protected under race discrimination provisions where the language requirement operates as a proxy for national origin)

regulatoryGuidance.title: Welsh Language Standards — Guidance for Organisations
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The Commissioner publishes sector-specific guidance on compliance with Welsh Language Standards, including employment-relevant operational standards covering recruitment advertising, workplace language policies, and staff training.

commonErrors: Assuming Welsh Language Standards only apply to public bodies — certain private sector organisations may also be subject to Standards through secondary legislation
Failing to include Welsh language in recruitment advertising where Standards require bilingual advertising
Not recognising that requiring Welsh language ability as an essential criterion for a role, without objective justification, may constitute indirect race discrimination under EqA 2010 s.19
Overlooking that employees who suffer detriment for using Welsh in the workplace may have claims under both the Welsh Language Measure and the Equality Act

sections.title: Equality Act 2010 (Statutory Duties) (Wales) Regulations 2011
sections.summary: Welsh public authorities are subject to enhanced specific equality duties under the Equality Act 2010 (Statutory Duties) (Wales) Regulations 2011 (SI 2011/1064). These are more extensive than the equivalent English duties and include: setting and publishing equality objectives, collecting and analysing equality data (including workforce data by protected characteristic), conducting equality impact assessments, and publishing an annual equality report. Employment-relevant requirements include equal pay auditing and workforce diversity monitoring.
sections.summaryCy: Mae awdurdodau cyhoeddus Cymreig yn ddarostyngedig i ddyletswyddau cydraddoldeb penodol estynedig o dan Ddeddf Cydraddoldeb 2010 (Dyletswyddau Statudol) (Cymru) Rheoliadau 2011.
sections.text: The Equality Act 2010 (Statutory Duties) (Wales) Regulations 2011 (SI 2011/1064) are made under sections 153 and 154 of the Equality Act 2010. They impose specific duties on Welsh public authorities listed in Schedule 1 to the Regulations, designed to enable better performance of the general equality duty in section 149 of the EqA 2010.
sections.keyPrinciple: The Welsh specific duties are MORE extensive than the English equivalent (Equality Act 2010 (Specific Duties) Regulations 2011 for England). Welsh public bodies must: set equality objectives every 4 years, collect and publish workforce equality data disaggregated by protected characteristic, conduct equality impact assessments on new and revised policies, and publish an annual equality report. The workforce data requirements are particularly significant — Welsh public bodies must collect, analyse and publish information about the protected characteristics of their employees.

leadingCases.name: R (Bracking) v Secretary of State for Work and Pensions
leadingCases.principle: The public sector equality duty requires 'rigorous' consideration of equality impacts, with the decision-maker aware of the duty and exercising it 'in substance, with rigour, and with an open mind'. A decision-maker must be aware of the duty to have due regard and must collect and consider sufficient information to enable compliance.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the duty under EqA 2010 s.149 is a duty of substance, not form. The decision-maker must be aware of the duty, must actively consider equality implications before and during the decision-making process, and must not treat it as a tick-box exercise. The court identified six principles governing compliance with the PSED.
leadingCases.significance: The leading EWCA authority on the general PSED (s.149) and by extension the specific duties which support it. The six Bracking principles apply equally to Welsh public bodies discharging their enhanced specific duties. Any equality impact assessment that does not satisfy the Bracking standard is vulnerable to judicial review.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Technical Guidance on the Public Sector Equality Duty: Wales
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: EHRC statutory technical guidance for Welsh public bodies on compliance with the general duty (s.149) and the Welsh specific duties. Covers workforce monitoring, equality impact assessments, equality objective setting, and annual reporting.

commonErrors: Assuming the Welsh duties are the same as the English duties — the Welsh regulations impose MORE extensive requirements, including mandatory equality impact assessments (not required in England), workforce data collection disaggregated by ALL protected characteristics (not just gender and ethnicity), and 4-yearly equality objective cycles (England requires only 'one or more' objectives)

Not collecting workforce data by protected characteristic — the Welsh specific duties require listed bodies to collect and analyse information about employees' protected characteristics for the purposes of the general duty. This includes monitoring recruitment, retention, promotion, and grievance/disciplinary outcomes by protected characteristic

Treating equality impact assessments as a post-decision formality — the Bracking principles require that the equality analysis takes place BEFORE the decision is made, with the decision-maker actively considering the analysis. A retrospective EIA is worthless

Publishing equality objectives without measurable outcomes — the Welsh duties require objectives that are specific, measurable, and capable of demonstrating progress. Vague aspirational statements do not satisfy the duty
refs: Equality Act 2010 (Statutory Duties) (Wales) Regulations 2011 (wsi/2011/1064)
EqA 2010 s.149 (public sector equality duty)
EqA 2010 s.153–154 (power to impose specific duties)
EHRC Technical Guidance: The Equality Duty in Wales

sections.title: Agriculture (Wales) Act 2014
sections.summary: The Agriculture (Wales) Act 2014 established the Agricultural Advisory Panel for Wales, which sets minimum terms and conditions for agricultural workers in Wales — including agricultural minimum wages (which exceed the national minimum wage), holiday entitlement, and other terms. This is a devolved power: agricultural wages in Wales are set by the Welsh Government, not the UK Government. The Agricultural Advisory Panel replaced the Agricultural Wages Board for England and Wales, which was abolished for England by the Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 but preserved for Wales.
sections.summaryCy: Sefydlodd Deddf Amaethyddiaeth (Cymru) 2014 y Panel Cynghori Amaethyddol i Gymru, sy'n gosod telerau ac amodau lleiafswm ar gyfer gweithwyr amaethyddol yng Nghymru — gan gynnwys cyflogau lleiafswm amaethyddol (sy'n uwch na'r isafswm cyflog cenedlaethol), hawl gwyliau, a thelerau eraill.
sections.text: The Agriculture (Wales) Act 2014 (anaw/2014/6) received Royal Assent on 30 July 2014. Part 3 establishes the Agricultural Advisory Panel for Wales and its power to make agricultural wages orders setting minimum rates of pay, holiday entitlement, sick pay, and other terms for agricultural workers in Wales.
sections.keyPrinciple: Agricultural employment in Wales has a separate minimum wage framework set by the Welsh Government through the Agricultural Advisory Panel — employers in the agricultural sector in Wales must comply with both the UK national minimum wage and the (higher) Welsh agricultural minimum wage.

currentRates.note: The Agricultural Minimum Wage Order (Wales) sets rates above the UK NMW for experienced agricultural workers. Rates are updated annually by the Welsh Government on advice from the Agricultural Advisory Panel for Wales. Employers must check the current order — failure to pay the agricultural minimum wage is a breach enforceable by HMRC.
currentRates.orderReference: Agricultural Minimum Wage Order (Wales) — current order should be verified annually at legislation.gov.uk
currentRates.gradeStructure: Grade A (initial grade), Grade B (standard worker), Grade C (lead worker), Grade D (craft grade), Grade E (senior craft grade), Grade F (management grade). Each grade has a separate hourly and overtime rate. Apprentice rates also specified.

refs: Agriculture (Wales) Act 2014 (anaw/2014/6)
Agricultural Advisory Panel for Wales — annual wages orders
NMWA 1998 (UK national minimum wage — the 'floor' below which agricultural wages may not fall)
Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 s.72 (abolished Agricultural Wages Board for England — does not apply to Wales)
commonErrors: Paying agricultural workers in Wales only the UK national minimum wage — the Welsh Agricultural Minimum Wage Order sets HIGHER rates for experienced workers (Grade B and above). Paying only NMW to a Grade C lead worker or above is a breach of the Welsh agricultural wages order

Not knowing that Wales retained agricultural wages regulation when England abolished it — the Agricultural Wages Board was abolished for England by ERRA 2013 s.72 but the Agriculture (Wales) Act 2014 preserved and replaced it with the Agricultural Advisory Panel for Wales. This is a genuine divergence between English and Welsh employment obligations

Failing to check the current Agricultural Minimum Wage Order annually — the Panel advises Welsh Ministers on rates which are updated by order. The rates, grade structure, and overtime provisions differ from the UK NMW framework and must be checked separately

Overlooking that the agricultural wages framework covers not just hourly rates but also holiday entitlement, sick pay, and other terms — these may be more generous than the statutory minimum under ERA 1996 and WTR 1998

sections.title: Social Partnership and Public Procurement (Wales) Act 2023
sections.titleCy: Deddf Partneriaeth Gymdeithasol a Chaffael Cyhoeddus (Cymru) 2023
sections.summary: The Social Partnership and Public Procurement (Wales) Act 2023 places a duty on specified public bodies in Wales to seek consensus with their workforce (through recognised trade unions or other representatives) on matters affecting workers. It also establishes the Social Partnership Council and imposes socially responsible procurement duties. The Act creates a statutory framework for social partnership — a model of cooperative industrial relations between government, employers, and trade unions — which has employment implications for public bodies and their supply chains in Wales.
sections.summaryCy: Mae Deddf Partneriaeth Gymdeithasol a Chaffael Cyhoeddus (Cymru) 2023 yn gosod dyletswydd ar gyrff cyhoeddus penodedig yng Nghymru i geisio consensws â'u gweithlu (trwy undebau llafur cydnabyddedig neu gynrychiolwyr eraill) ar faterion sy'n effeithio ar weithwyr.
sections.text: The Social Partnership and Public Procurement (Wales) Act 2023 received Royal Assent on 24 May 2023. Part 1 establishes the social partnership duty on specified public bodies. Section 2 requires bodies to seek consensus or compromise with their recognised trade unions on specified matters affecting workers.
refs: Social Partnership and Public Procurement (Wales) Act 2023 (asc/2023/1)
Well-being of Future Generations (Wales) Act 2015 (the social partnership duty interacts with the well-being duty)
TULRCA 1992 (recognition provisions — interact with the social partnership duty)
Public Contracts Regulations 2015 (procurement framework within which the Welsh Act operates)
commonErrors: Assuming the social partnership duty only applies where a trade union is recognised — the duty requires engagement with workforce representatives regardless of union recognition status. Where no union is recognised, the body must establish other means of workforce engagement

Confusing the social partnership duty with collective bargaining under TULRCA 1992 — social partnership is a broader concept requiring consensus-seeking on strategic matters affecting workers, not just traditional collective bargaining subjects (pay, hours, holidays)
Not recognising that the social partnership duty extends to procurement decisions — the Act requires socially responsible procurement, which may include workforce-related conditions in contracts

sections.title: Well-being of Future Generations (Wales) Act 2015
sections.summary: The Well-being of Future Generations (Wales) Act 2015 imposes duties on specified public bodies to carry out sustainable development by setting well-being objectives and taking reasonable steps to meet them. The Act's seven well-being goals include 'a more equal Wales' and 'a Wales of cohesive communities'. These duties have employment implications: public bodies must consider the well-being of their workforce and the wider community impact of employment decisions, procurement, and service delivery. The Future Generations Commissioner for Wales monitors compliance.
sections.summaryCy: Mae Deddf Llesiant Cenedlaethau'r Dyfodol (Cymru) 2015 yn gosod dyletswyddau ar gyrff cyhoeddus penodedig i gynnal datblygiad cynaliadwy drwy osod amcanion llesiant a chymryd camau rhesymol i'w cyflawni.
sections.text: The Well-being of Future Generations (Wales) Act 2015 (anaw/2015/2) received Royal Assent on 29 April 2015. Section 3 sets out seven well-being goals. Section 5 requires each public body to set and publish well-being objectives designed to maximise its contribution to achieving the well-being goals.
refs: Well-being of Future Generations (Wales) Act 2015 (anaw/2015/2)
Future Generations Commissioner for Wales — oversight body
Social Partnership and Public Procurement (Wales) Act 2023 (interacts with well-being duties)
EqA 2010 s.149 (public sector equality duty — complementary obligation)
commonErrors: Treating the well-being duty as purely environmental — the seven goals include 'a more equal Wales' and 'a Wales of cohesive communities', which have direct workforce implications including fair work practices, community impact assessments for large-scale redundancies, and procurement decisions affecting local employment

Not applying the five ways of working (long-term, integration, involvement, collaboration, prevention) to employment decisions — restructurings, workforce planning, and outsourcing decisions all engage the duty

Overlooking the Future Generations Commissioner's power to review and report on compliance — the Commissioner's reports have criticised Welsh public bodies for failing to embed well-being considerations in workforce decisions

sections.title: Equality Act 2010 s.1 — Socio-economic Duty (commenced in Wales only)
sections.titleCy: Deddf Cydraddoldeb 2010 a.1 — Dyletswydd Economaidd-Gymdeithasol (wedi'i gychwyn yng Nghymru yn unig)
sections.summary: Section 1 of the Equality Act 2010 imposes a duty on specified public bodies to have due regard to the desirability of reducing the inequalities of outcome which result from socio-economic disadvantage when making decisions of a strategic nature. This section was commenced in Wales on 31 March 2021 by the Equality Act 2010 (Commencement No. 15) (Wales) Order 2021 — but has NOT been commenced in England. It applies to Welsh Ministers, local authorities in Wales, local health boards, NHS trusts in Wales, and other specified Welsh public bodies. Employment-relevant implications include strategic decisions about pay policies, recruitment from deprived areas, procurement requirements, and workforce development programmes.
sections.summaryCy: Mae adran 1 o Ddeddf Cydraddoldeb 2010 yn gosod dyletswydd ar gyrff cyhoeddus penodedig i roi sylw dyledus i ddymunoldeb lleihau'r anghydraddoldebau canlyniad sy'n deillio o anfantais economaidd-gymdeithasol wrth wneud penderfyniadau o natur strategol. Cychwynnwyd yr adran hon yng Nghymru ar 31 Mawrth 2021 — ond NID yw wedi'i gychwyn yn Lloegr.
sections.text:
Section 1(1) of the Equality Act 2010 provides: An authority to which this section applies must, when making decisions of a strategic nature about how to exercise its functions, have due regard to the desirability of exercising them in a way that is designed to reduce the inequalities of outcome which result from socio-economic disadvantage.

The Equality Act 2010 (Commencement No. 15) (Wales) Order 2021 (SI 2021/298 (W.78)) commenced s.1 in Wales on 31 March 2021.
sections.keyPrinciple: This is a Wales-only duty — it has not been commenced in England. Welsh public bodies must consider socio-economic disadvantage when making strategic decisions, which includes strategic employment decisions such as pay structures, recruitment strategies, and workforce planning.
refs: EqA 2010 s.1 (socio-economic duty)
Equality Act 2010 (Commencement No. 15) (Wales) Order 2021 (SI 2021/298 (W.78))
EqA 2010 s.149 (public sector equality duty — separate and complementary)
Welsh Government Guidance: A More Equal Wales
commonErrors: Not knowing this duty exists — EqA 2010 s.1 was commenced in Wales on 31 March 2021 but has NOT been commenced in England. Many HR professionals trained on English practice are unaware it applies in Wales

Misunderstanding what constitutes a 'strategic decision' — the duty applies to decisions of a strategic nature, not operational day-to-day decisions. Strategic employment decisions include: pay policy reviews, workforce restructuring, recruitment strategy changes, procurement decisions affecting workforce terms, and organisational development programmes

Treating the duty as identical to the PSED in s.149 — the socio-economic duty is separate and additional. It focuses specifically on reducing inequalities of OUTCOME resulting from socio-economic disadvantage, which requires different analysis from the protected characteristics focus of s.149

Not documenting the due regard given to socio-economic disadvantage in strategic employment decisions — as with the PSED, the duty requires demonstrable due regard, not just awareness. Decision papers should record the analysis

sections.title: Nurse Staffing Levels (Wales) Act 2016
sections.summary: The Nurse Staffing Levels (Wales) Act 2016 imposes a statutory duty on local health boards and NHS trusts in Wales to calculate and maintain nurse staffing levels sufficient to provide care to patients sensitively. This is the first legislation of its kind in Europe. For employers, it creates legally binding minimum staffing requirements that directly affect workforce planning, recruitment strategy, agency usage, working hours, and overtime.
sections.summaryCy: Mae Deddf Lefelau Staffio Nyrsio (Cymru) 2016 yn gosod dyletswydd statudol ar fyrddau iechyd lleol ac ymddiriedolaethau'r GIG yng Nghymru i gyfrifo a chynnal lefelau staffio nyrsio sy'n ddigonol i ddarparu gofal i gleifion yn sensitif.
sections.text: The Nurse Staffing Levels (Wales) Act 2016 (anaw/2016/5) received Royal Assent on 21 March 2016. Section 25A (inserted into the National Health Service (Wales) Act 2006) provides that it is the duty of each local health board and NHS trust to have regard to the importance of providing sufficient nurses to allow the nurses time to care for patients sensitively.
refs: Nurse Staffing Levels (Wales) Act 2016 (anaw/2016/5)
National Health Service (Wales) Act 2006 s.25A–25E (inserted by the 2016 Act)
Nurse Staffing Levels (Wales) Regulations 2021 (SI 2021/519 (W.127))
commonErrors: Assuming nurse staffing levels are discretionary clinical guidance — in Wales they are a statutory duty enforceable through the regulatory framework. Non-compliance exposes NHS bodies to regulatory action and potential negligence liability

Not recognising the workforce planning implications — the statutory staffing method requires NHS bodies to calculate and maintain specific nurse-to-patient ratios, which directly constrains decisions about recruitment freezes, agency caps, and skill-mix changes

Overlooking the 2021 Regulations extending scope — the original Act applied to adult acute medical and surgical wards only; the 2021 Regulations extended coverage to all adult acute inpatient wards, significantly increasing the workforce planning obligation

sections.title: Additional Learning Needs and Education Tribunal (Wales) Act 2018
sections.titleCy: Deddf Anghenion Dysgu Ychwanegol a'r Tribiwnlys Addysg (Cymru) 2018
sections.summary: The Additional Learning Needs and Education Tribunal (Wales) Act 2018 replaces the special educational needs (SEN) framework in Wales with a new system of Additional Learning Needs (ALN). For employers in the education sector in Wales, this creates significant obligations: schools, further education institutions, and local authorities must identify and support learners with ALN through Individual Development Plans (IDPs). Staff must be trained in the new ALN framework, Additional Learning Needs Co-ordinators (ALNCos) must be appointed with dedicated time, and educational psychologists and specialist teachers face changed role definitions. The Act commenced in phases from September 2021.
sections.summaryCy: Mae Deddf Anghenion Dysgu Ychwanegol a'r Tribiwnlys Addysg (Cymru) 2018 yn disodli'r fframwaith anghenion addysgol arbennig (AAA) yng Nghymru gyda system newydd o Anghenion Dysgu Ychwanegol (ADY). I gyflogwyr yn y sector addysg yng Nghymru, mae hyn yn creu rhwymedigaethau sylweddol.
sections.text: The Additional Learning Needs and Education Tribunal (Wales) Act 2018 (anaw/2018/2) received Royal Assent on 24 January 2018. It replaces the framework established by the Education Act 1996 Part IV and the SEN Code of Practice for Wales. The Act introduces: mandatory Additional Learning Needs Co-ordinator (ALNCo) roles in every maintained school and further education institution; Individual Development Plans (IDPs) replacing Statements of SEN; a unified system covering learners aged 0–25; and a reformed Education Tribunal for Wales.
refs: Additional Learning Needs and Education Tribunal (Wales) Act 2018 (anaw/2018/2)
ALN Code for Wales 2021 (statutory code under the Act)
Education Act 1996 Part IV (replaced in Wales by this Act)
Additional Learning Needs (Wales) Regulations 2021 (SI 2021/401 (W.132))
commonErrors: Not recognising the mandatory ALNCo appointment requirement — every maintained school and FE institution in Wales must appoint an Additional Learning Needs Co-ordinator with dedicated time for the role. This is a statutory duty, not discretionary

Failing to budget for ALN training — the Act and Code require all education staff to understand the ALN framework, with enhanced training for ALNCos, SENCos transitioning to the new system, and teaching assistants supporting learners with IDPs

Overlooking the 0–25 age range — the ALN system covers learners from birth to 25, which means post-16 education providers (FE colleges, sixth forms) have obligations they may not have had under the old SEN framework

sections.title: Health and Social Care (Quality and Engagement) (Wales) Act 2020
sections.titleCy: Deddf Iechyd a Gofal Cymdeithasol (Ansawdd ac Ymgysylltu) (Cymru) 2020
sections.summary: The Health and Social Care (Quality and Engagement) (Wales) Act 2020 places a duty of quality on NHS bodies in Wales, establishes a duty of candour requiring openness when treatment or care goes wrong, and strengthens the citizen voice body. For employers in NHS and social care in Wales, the Act creates specific workforce obligations: duty of candour training, quality governance embedding, and organisational cultures supporting open disclosure. Employees raising quality concerns under the duty of candour framework are protected, and organisational responses to adverse incidents must prioritise learning over blame.
sections.summaryCy: Mae Deddf Iechyd a Gofal Cymdeithasol (Ansawdd ac Ymgysylltu) (Cymru) 2020 yn gosod dyletswydd ansawdd ar gyrff y GIG yng Nghymru, yn sefydlu dyletswydd o ddidwylledd, ac yn cryfhau'r corff llais y dinesydd ar gyfer iechyd a gofal cymdeithasol.
sections.text: The Health and Social Care (Quality and Engagement) (Wales) Act 2020 (asc/2020/1) received Royal Assent on 1 June 2020. Part 1 places a duty of quality on Welsh Ministers and NHS bodies. Part 2 establishes a duty of candour requiring openness when treatment or care provided goes wrong. Part 3 establishes Llais (Citizen Voice Body for Health and Social Care).
refs: Health and Social Care (Quality and Engagement) (Wales) Act 2020 (asc/2020/1)
ERA 1996 Part IVA (whistleblowing — complementary protection for quality concerns)
Nurse Staffing Levels (Wales) Act 2016 (complementary workforce obligation)
commonErrors: Treating the duty of candour as purely clinical governance — it has direct employment implications including training, disciplinary procedure modifications, and whistleblowing protection
Not recognising that an employee raising a quality concern under the duty of candour may simultaneously be making a protected disclosure under ERA 1996 Part IVA — both regimes may apply
